Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Frans de Waal

1948 – 2024

Primate cognition, emotion, and the continuity between human and animal minds.

Frans de Waal did not write a single, systematic treatise called “A Philosophy of Mind.” His work is instead distributed across primatology, comparative psychology, ethology, moral psychology, and public argument against human exceptionalism. But a coherent philosophy can be reconstructed from it. The central thesis is best described as comparative affective naturalism: minds are embodied, affective, social, evolved, and distributed across species in different configurations; human distinctiveness is real, but it is not a metaphysical break between ensouled mind and soulless mechanism. De Waal’s targets are not only bad experiments and bad animal psychology, but also a theological-philosophical inheritance in which “soul,” “reason,” “morality,” and “consciousness” are used to wall human beings off from the rest of animal life. He called this wall-building “anthropodenial,” a refusal to see humanlike traits in animals and animal-like traits in ourselves.

The strongest version of de Waal’s view is not “animals are just like humans.” It is subtler: because human beings are animals, and because many other animals are our evolutionary relatives, the default scientific posture should be continuity unless there is evidence for discontinuity. He did not recommend sentimental projection. He recommended informed, species-sensitive comparison: attend to natural history, sensory worlds, social ecology, phylogenetic closeness, and experimental context before deciding what a behavior means. The result is a philosophy of mind that rejects both naïve anthropomorphism and mechanistic anthropodenial.

1. The soul as boundary-marker: what de Waal is really attacking

De Waal’s comments on the soul are not incidental. They are one of the deep background conditions of his entire project. He repeatedly treats the old human/animal divide as a fusion of Cartesian mechanism and Western religious exceptionalism: humans have souls, animals do not; humans have interiority, animals are machines; humans have morality, animals have instinct. In his “anthropodenial” essay, he explicitly connects this divide to the Cartesian image of humans as ensouled and animals as automata. In French interviews, he pushes the same diagnosis further: Western thought often accepts evolution “up to the neck,” as if the body evolved but the mind remained of another essence.

This does not mean de Waal gives a metaphysical disproof of the soul. His argument is more empirical and genealogical. He asks what work the concept of soul has done in Western discourse. In the tradition he criticizes, “soul” often functions as an exclusionary device: it reserves inwardness, subjectivity, moral standing, and spiritual value for humans alone. De Waal’s evidence attacks that exclusionary use. If animals show grief, reconciliation, empathy, planning, social strategy, emotional regulation, inequity aversion, and self-recognition, then the claim that animals are inwardly empty becomes scientifically implausible.

A careful theological reading should therefore distinguish two propositions. The first is “animals are soulless machines without inner life.” De Waal’s whole body of work is a sustained refutation of that proposition. The second is “human beings have a distinctive rational, spiritual, or theological vocation.” De Waal’s science does not directly refute every possible version of that claim. What it does refute is the move from human distinctiveness to animal emptiness. The theological issue is not whether humans may be special in some respects; it is whether human specialness requires denying mind, emotion, agency, or proto-morality to other animals. De Waal’s answer is no.

This point becomes clearer when de Waal compares Western and non-Western religious imaginaries. In a French interview, he suggests that resistance to Darwinism was weaker in some Buddhist and Hindu contexts partly because the idea that a soul may circulate between human and animal made the human/animal divide less absolute. He also notes that Chinese and Indian primate folklore contained named, character-bearing primates long before Western primatology was comfortable treating apes as minded beings. Whether or not one accepts the whole cultural explanation, the philosophical point is significant: theological imagery shapes what scientists and philosophers are prepared to perceive.

In that sense, de Waal’s “soul” criticism is not anti-theological trivia. It is epistemological. A culture that believes animals are soulless mechanisms will design poorer experiments, interpret animal behavior more narrowly, and treat evidence of animal feeling as dangerous sentimentality. A culture that expects continuity between human and animal life may see more accurately. De Waal’s project is therefore not merely biological; it is a critique of a theological-metaphysical filter on perception.

2. Anthropodenial and controlled anthropomorphism

The methodological heart of de Waal’s philosophy is the balance between anthropomorphism and anthropodenial. Anthropomorphism is the risk of projecting human thoughts and feelings where they do not belong. Anthropodenial is the opposite risk: refusing to recognize genuine similarities because they threaten human exceptionalism. De Waal’s claim is that modern science became hypersensitive to the first danger and under-sensitive to the second. In the behaviorist period, scientists often avoided words such as fear, desire, consolation, intention, or jealousy, replacing them with externalized functional descriptions. De Waal judged that linguistic austerity to be misleading when it prevented scientists from testing hypotheses about mental life.

His rule is close to Humean analogy and inference to the best explanation. If closely related species behave similarly in similar circumstances, it is often more parsimonious to assume similar underlying psychology than to invent radically different mechanisms. De Waal used this reasoning for chimpanzees, bonobos, capuchins, elephants, dogs, and other social animals, while recognizing that confidence declines as phylogenetic and ecological distance increases. This is why he could say that we never know exactly what an animal feels, while also saying that we never know exactly what another human feels either. Other minds are always inferred.

This is not a license for indiscriminate projection. De Waal’s preferred anthropomorphism is disciplined by Umwelt, the animal’s own perceptual and ecological world. In his anthropodenial essay, he says that anthropomorphic interpretation must be guided by species-specific habits and natural history. A human smile, a rhesus macaque bared-teeth display, and a chimpanzee vocalization cannot be interpreted by the same rule simply because they look superficially alike. The more closely related the species and the more similar the sensory and social context, the stronger the anthropomorphic inference becomes.

This gives de Waal’s philosophy of mind a distinctive epistemology: animal minds are not directly observed, but neither are human minds. We infer mindedness from behavior, physiology, social context, neural similarity, developmental continuity, and evolutionary relationship. To demand impossible certainty for animal minds while accepting everyday inference for human minds is, for de Waal, a double standard.

3. Species-specific intelligence: against the single ladder of mind

De Waal rejects the idea that cognition can be ranked on a single scale with humans at the top and all other species below. The right question is not whether an octopus, crow, elephant, chimpanzee, or dog is “as smart as a human,” but how that animal processes information and solves the problems posed by its own world. In an interview on animal intelligence, he defines cognition broadly as information processing for environmental problem-solving and criticizes experiments that test animals according to human-centered standards.

His favorite examples often involve failed experiments that reveal more about human bias than animal incapacity. Monkeys tested on human faces may perform poorly, but when tested on faces of their own species, they show capacities that the original experiment missed. In a French interview, de Waal uses this case to argue that primate cognition had only begun to be explored properly, and that corvids, dolphins, and elephants also show sophisticated forms of intelligence.

This methodological point has major philosophical implications. Intelligence is not a substance possessed in greater or lesser quantity along a single human-centered ladder. It is a family of adaptive capacities: memory, planning, social inference, tool use, cooperation, inhibition, emotional regulation, communication, spatial navigation, and more. Human intelligence is one configuration, not the measure of all possible mind.

The same principle affects his view of consciousness. De Waal does not begin with an abstract definition of consciousness and then ask which animals meet it. He begins with capacities that in humans are closely associated with consciousness: planning, episodic memory, self-control, emotional awareness, social perspective-taking, and self-recognition. When animals show these capacities in ecologically appropriate ways, consciousness becomes a plausible explanation.

4. Emotion and feeling: the embodied basis of mind

De Waal draws an important distinction between emotions and feelings. Emotions are bodily and behavioral states: facial expression, posture, heart rate, blood pressure, hormonal response, avoidance, approach, consolation, aggression, freezing, grooming, and so forth. Feelings are the subjective experience of those states. We can measure the former more directly than the latter. But this is true for humans too: a scientist can measure another person’s facial expression and physiology, but cannot directly access the person’s felt experience.

This distinction allows de Waal to be cautious without being skeptical. He does not claim to read animal feelings with certainty. He says that when animals have similar bodies, brains, expressions, social attachments, and behavioral responses, it is reasonable to infer similar feelings. In the final interview published by Skeptic, he explicitly connects this line of thought to Darwin’s work on emotional expression: if expressive behavior is shared across species, the best explanation may be shared emotional experience, not merely empty performance.

For de Waal, emotion is not the irrational opposite of cognition. Emotion organizes action. Fear prepares the body, but cognition selects an appropriate response; emotion and cognition cooperate. This is a crucial anti-Cartesian and anti-Kantian move. The mind is not a rational executive sitting above animal impulse. It is an integrated organismic system in which perception, affect, memory, motivation, and action are interdependent.

This also explains why de Waal is not a simple “instinct versus reason” thinker. Emotions are not blind reflexes; they are structured, context-sensitive, and socially modulated. Apes, for example, can show self-control in delayed-gratification tasks, use distraction, and regulate their emotions. In a Dutch interview, de Waal even says great apes have a form of free will, grounding that claim in self-control rather than in metaphysical libertarianism.

His implied theory of mind is therefore embodied and affective. A mind is not first a language-using rational consciousness that later acquires emotion. A mind is an organism’s affectively charged orientation toward its world. Language may transform that mind, but it does not create inwardness from nothing.

5. Consciousness: graded, inferred, and plural

De Waal is not primarily a philosopher of the “hard problem” of consciousness. He does not offer a theory of qualia, panpsychism, physicalism, or representational content in the technical analytic sense. His contribution is different: he gives a comparative scientific framework for deciding when consciousness should be attributed to nonhuman animals. His answer is cautious but affirmative. He says animals have “some level” of consciousness, and that we should look for human capacities that seem impossible without consciousness, then ask whether animals display them.

The relevant indicators include planning, episodic memory, social understanding, self-control, emotion regulation, and self-recognition. De Waal points to future-oriented planning in apes and birds, episodic memory in animals, and experimental evidence that apes can delay gratification and regulate affect. These do not prove that animal consciousness is identical to human consciousness. They support a graded model in which different animals possess different layers or dimensions of awareness.

Mirror self-recognition is one important but limited case. De Waal was a coauthor on the 2006 elephant mirror self-recognition study, which placed elephants alongside apes and dolphins in discussions of bodily self-awareness. The philosophical significance is not that a mirror test exhausts selfhood; it clearly does not. Rather, it weakens the assumption that reflective bodily self-recognition is uniquely human or uniquely primate.

A de Waalian theory of consciousness should therefore be multidimensional. There is affective consciousness: feeling fear, distress, comfort, attachment, grief, or pleasure. There is bodily self-awareness: distinguishing one’s own body from the environment. There is social consciousness: tracking alliances, rank, kinship, reconciliation, and the emotional states of others. There is temporal consciousness: remembering past events and anticipating future ones. There is norm-sensitive consciousness: expecting reciprocity, reacting to unfair treatment, and participating in social rules. Human beings add powerful symbolic, linguistic, institutional, and reflective layers, but these are elaborations on older capacities rather than replacements for them.

This makes de Waal an opponent of all-or-nothing consciousness. The question is not “Which species possess consciousness in the full human sense?” The better question is “Which forms of awareness, affect, self-relation, and social understanding are present in this species, in this ecology, under these conditions?” That question is more scientifically tractable and less distorted by human pride.

6. The social mind: politics, reconciliation, and relational intelligence

De Waal’s earliest major fame came from showing that chimpanzee social life was politically structured. Chimpanzees form coalitions, maintain rank relationships, cultivate allies, reconcile after conflict, and use social intelligence to navigate power. In interviews, he compared chimpanzee coalitions and diplomacy to human politics, not because chimpanzees have parliaments, but because power among primates is relational, strategic, and socially mediated.

His 1979 work with Angeline van Roosmalen on reconciliation and consolation among chimpanzees is foundational for this social philosophy of mind. They observed that former opponents often made peaceful body contact soon after conflict, including gestures described as kisses and embraces, and they interpreted these contacts as serving social homeostasis. The point is not merely that animals “make up.” It is that conflict repair presupposes social memory, relationship value, emotional modulation, and sensitivity to the difference between hostile and affiliative contact.

Consolation is even more philosophically suggestive. When a bystander comforts a distressed individual, the behavior is not reducible to immediate self-interest in any obvious way. It suggests emotional contagion, concern, social attachment, and perhaps some level of perspective-taking. De Waal repeatedly treats consolation as one of the strongest signs that mammalian social life contains the building blocks of moral psychology.

This is why de Waal’s mind is not an isolated Cartesian subject. It is relational. Minds emerge and operate within networks of rank, kinship, alliance, rivalry, grooming, play, sex, parenting, coalition, punishment, reconciliation, and care. To understand a chimpanzee mind, one must understand chimpanzee society. To understand a human mind, one must also understand primate society, even if human society has been transformed by language, institutions, and culture.

7. Empathy: the “Russian doll” model of moral psychology

Empathy is the center of de Waal’s moral psychology and one of the main bridges between his philosophy of mind and his philosophy of morality. He treats empathy not as a purely human, rational, moral achievement, but as a layered mammalian capacity. Its most basic layer is emotional contagion: one body resonates with another’s affective state. More complex layers include sympathetic concern, targeted helping, and perspective-taking. De Waal’s “Russian doll” model presents these layers as nested rather than mutually exclusive: higher forms depend on lower affective mechanisms.

In Dutch and French interviews, he says basic empathy is found in all mammals, while more complex forms appear especially in elephants, apes, dolphins, and other highly social animals. He links the evolutionary origin of empathy to mammalian maternal care: offspring need responsive caregivers, and caregivers need sensitivity to offspring distress. From there, empathy can expand beyond mother-infant relations to kin, allies, group members, strangers, and sometimes even enemies.

This model is philosophically important because it reverses a common intellectualist picture. On the intellectualist picture, one first understands another mind conceptually and then chooses to care. On de Waal’s picture, affective resonance comes first. Cognition can refine, inhibit, redirect, universalize, or exploit empathy, but it is not the original source of concern. Moral life begins in embodied social responsiveness.

De Waal is not naïve about empathy. In the French interview, he notes that empathy has a dark side: understanding another’s feelings can enable manipulation, intimidation, torture, or marketing. This is an important qualification. Empathy is not identical with goodness. It is a psychological capacity that can support care, but also strategic cruelty.

The full de Waalian view is therefore: empathy is necessary for morality but not sufficient for morality. Without concern for others, moral life cannot begin. But concern must be shaped by norms, restraint, reciprocity, fairness, social learning, and—in humans—language, justification, and institutions.

8. Morality from below: against “veneer theory”

De Waal’s best-known moral-philosophical polemic is against what he calls veneer theory. Veneer theory says that human morality is a thin cultural, religious, or rational layer spread over a basically selfish, brutish animal nature. De Waal rejects this as biologically false and philosophically misleading. Humans have cruel and selfish tendencies, but they also have evolved tendencies toward attachment, sympathy, reciprocity, reconciliation, cooperation, and fairness. These are not artificial coatings on an otherwise antisocial animal; they are part of primate and mammalian nature.

His book Primates and Philosophers explicitly asks why “the animal in us” is blamed for brutality but not credited for moral concern. Its central thesis is that human morality has biological foundations in care for the vulnerable, reciprocal exchange, empathy, and social regulation. The book also includes responses from philosophers such as Peter Singer, Christine Korsgaard, Philip Kitcher, and Robert Wright, which is important because de Waal’s view was immediately placed in dialogue with debates about normativity, obligation, and the difference between human morality and animal proto-morality.

His earlier book Good Natured similarly asks readers to reconsider human morality in light of moral aspects of animal behavior, including reciprocity, obligation-like expectations, community concern, and social rules. De Waal is careful not to collapse all animal social behavior into full human morality. Rather, he argues that the ingredients of morality are older than humanity.

The capuchin inequity-aversion experiments are central here. In the famous “unequal pay” work, capuchins reacted negatively when another monkey received a better reward for comparable work. A later clarification emphasized that the claim was not that capuchins possess a full human concept of justice, but that they show evolutionary precursors of inequity aversion, especially disadvantageous inequity aversion. This is exactly the kind of careful distinction de Waal’s philosophy requires: animals may not have explicit moral principles, but they can have emotionally charged expectations about treatment, exchange, and social comparison.

The result is a bottom-up account of morality. Moral systems do not descend fully formed from reason, religion, or law. They grow out of social emotions and practices: attachment, empathy, reciprocity, protest, reconciliation, punishment, sharing, alliance, and care. Human morality becomes distinctive when these are linguistically articulated, symbolically generalized, institutionally stabilized, and sometimes universalized beyond kin and group. But the motivational roots are older.

9. Hume against Kant: moral sentimentalism and rational elaboration

De Waal frequently aligns himself more with Hume than with Kant. In the French interview, he contrasts a Kantian top-down morality grounded in reason and logical principle with a more Humean view in which moral judgment begins in feeling and is later rationalized. In another interview, he says philosophers often overemphasize justification and underestimate motivation: people may give reasons after the fact, but their moral impulses commonly arise from social emotions.

This is not anti-rationalism in the crude sense. De Waal does not deny that reason matters. He denies that reason is the original source of moral motivation. Reason can extend sympathy, formalize rules, detect inconsistency, create institutions, and criticize parochial bias. But if an individual had no concern for others at all, reason alone would not generate morality. As he puts it in the “Why Are We Here?” interview, empathy is foundational because without interest in others one cannot really be moral.

His Dutch interview adds a crucial limit. De Waal acknowledges that human morality often aims at objectivity beyond immediate relationships, while animal social concern usually remains embedded in concrete relationships. He says he sees no evidence that chimpanzees strive for an objective moral system beyond their social world. This is a major concession and should be built into any rigorous reconstruction. De Waal’s view is not that chimpanzees are Kantians. It is that Kantian moral systems are elaborations built on older affective and social capacities.

The de Waalian formula would be: moral motivation is older than moral justification; moral emotions are older than moral principles; proto-normativity is older than explicit ethics. Human beings do not transcend animality by becoming moral. They become morally articulate animals.

10. Free will, agency, and self-control

De Waal’s remarks on free will are brief but revealing. In the Dutch interview titled “Great apes have a free will,” he grounds the claim in self-control. Some apes can delay gratification in marshmallow-like tasks, using distraction and inhibition to wait for a better outcome. This is not a metaphysical proof of libertarian free will. It is a compatibilist, behavioral, and cognitive conception of agency: an animal has more freedom when it can inhibit immediate impulse, anticipate a future state, and regulate its own affective system.

This gives us a de Waalian account of agency. Agency is not absolute uncaused choice. It is graded self-regulation within an embodied organism. A creature becomes more agentic as it can delay, plan, negotiate, reconcile, deceive, cooperate, choose allies, repair relationships, and manage emotions. Human freedom is more symbolically mediated and institutionally complex, but it is not metaphysically discontinuous from animal self-control.

This view also bears on artificial intelligence and computational theories of mind. Since de Waal treats emotion as an organizing bodily system rather than mere data processing, he implicitly resists a purely computational account of mind. A system that calculates without affective embodiment may simulate some cognitive outputs, but it lacks the mammalian architecture in which emotion, motivation, body, and social attachment co-produce minded behavior. His final interview emphasizes that emotion and cognition work together in animals rather than occupying separate domains.

11. Religion: not the source of morality, not merely poison

De Waal’s theology-adjacent claims are unusually balanced. He was an atheist or nonbeliever, but not a militant anti-religious writer. Reviews and interviews describe him as having little interest in disproving God and substantial interest in why religion exists as a human universal. In one interview, he says the biologist’s question is not whether God exists but why religions exist and what they do for human societies.

His central claim is that morality is older than religion. Humans existed long before current world religions, and the social ingredients of morality—care, fairness, reciprocity, rules, and concern—are older still. Religion, on this view, did not create morality ex nihilo. It codified, amplified, supervised, symbolized, and sometimes redirected moral tendencies that were already present. He suggests that divine surveillance may have helped large-scale societies enforce cooperation among people who were no longer embedded in small face-to-face communities.

The French publisher’s presentation of Le Bonobo, Dieu et nous captures this bottom-up thesis: human morality is not imposed from above but arises from within, through evolved capacities for cooperation, empathy, fairness, and social life. Religion is described there as a late arrival added to natural instincts, while de Waal is also presented as someone who does not simply despise religion and who asks what role religion may still play.

This is why de Waal’s criticism of the “New Atheist” style matters. He rejects the religious monopoly on morality, but he also rejects the idea that religion is nothing but intellectual error or social poison. In the “Why Are We Here?” interview, he says religion is broader than an explanatory system; for many people it gives guidance about life, while science does not by itself tell us what a good life is.

The theological significance is substantial. De Waal offers neither Christian theology nor secular reductionism in the crudest sense. He offers a natural history of moral motivation and religious function. Religion is neither the foundation of empathy nor an irrelevant distraction. It is a human cultural system that works on, organizes, narrates, and sometimes distorts evolved social emotions.

12. Animal proto-religion? Awe, death, ritual, and the limits of evidence

De Waal sometimes discusses behaviors that invite comparison with religious precursors: chimpanzee “rain dances,” responses to death, bonobo mourning, and ritual-like social displays. German reception of The Bonobo and the Atheist foregrounded this dimension, describing de Waal as asking whether such behaviors might be preforms of religiosity or natural roots of practices later elaborated into religion.

A rigorous reconstruction should treat this cautiously. De Waal is not saying chimpanzees have theology, doctrines, gods, priesthoods, or metaphysical beliefs. The better interpretation is that certain affective and social dispositions—arousal before natural forces, attention to death, group synchrony, ritualized display, and emotional contagion—may be preconditions out of which human religion later develops. As with morality, the claim is genealogical: human religion may elaborate older affective and social capacities rather than appear from nowhere.

This also prevents a simplistic secularization narrative. Religion is not merely false explanation replaced by science. It is tied to awe, fear, mourning, belonging, authority, symbolism, and moral orientation. De Waal’s animal comparisons do not explain religion away; they relocate parts of religion in the broader evolution of social mammals and primates.

13. Human distinctiveness: real, but relocated

De Waal is not a flat continuity theorist who denies all human uniqueness. In the Dutch interview, when asked in a Kantian frame what is distinctive about human beings, he names capacities such as dance, music, mastery of fire, language, and supernatural belief, while emphasizing that continuity with evolutionary relatives impresses him more than differences. He repeatedly treats language as especially important because symbolic communication transforms human social life.

The key is that de Waal relocates human uniqueness. Humans are not unique because only humans have minds, emotions, morality, culture, politics, or consciousness. Versions or precursors of these exist elsewhere. Humans are distinctive because they combine primate social-emotional capacities with symbolic language, cumulative culture, explicit norm systems, institutions, large-scale cooperation, historical memory, theology, science, and abstract justification.

This relocation matters for philosophy of mind. Human consciousness is not the first consciousness; it is a linguistically and culturally amplified consciousness. Human morality is not the first concern for others; it is concern transformed by symbols, rules, narratives, and institutions. Human religion is not the first awe or attachment; it is affective-social life organized around gods, rituals, doctrines, and sacred meanings.

14. Multilingual reception: what non-English sources add

The Dutch reception is philosophically valuable because interviewers often ask de Waal in explicitly philosophical terms. The Filosofie Magazine interview frames him through Kant’s four questions and elicits some of his clearest comments on knowledge of animal feeling, free will in apes, Hume versus Kant, the limits of animal morality, and the universality of supernatural belief. That Dutch material makes especially clear that de Waal is not just doing popular science; he is intervening in long-standing debates about reason, moral motivation, hope, and human nature.

The French material adds the most explicit theological-cultural diagnosis. De Waal’s French interview emphasizes the Western habit of accepting evolution for the body but not for the mind, and contrasts that with traditions in which the human/animal soul boundary is less rigid. The French presentation of Le Bonobo, Dieu et nous also highlights the phrase “morality from below,” the late arrival of religion, and de Waal’s refusal to treat religion merely as contemptible.

The Spanish-language material often emphasizes ethical and ecological consequences. In a BBVA interview, de Waal presents humans as primates at the social and emotional level, while noting that language and certain forms of intelligence distinguish us. The same interview connects human exceptionalism to destructive attitudes toward animals and nature, arguing that educators and philosophers need to rethink the assumption that humans are simply better or separate. A Spanish obituary-style piece similarly describes his work as dismantling theories that attributed emotions exclusively to humans and recalls the older image of animals as soulless machines.

The German reception is useful because it preserves tensions. Some German reviews foreground de Waal’s critique of militant atheism and his interest in peaceful coexistence between science and religion, while others worry that the equation of pro-social animal behavior with morality can become imprecise. That is a fair philosophical pressure point: de Waal’s strongest view is not that all pro-social behavior is morality, but that pro-social capacities are evolutionary and psychological preconditions for morality.

Portuguese-language reception often frames his work through “soul,” “reason,” and “sentience,” which is revealing even when the sources are less academically central. It shows that in some reception contexts de Waal is immediately read as a thinker who destabilizes the inherited triad “soul/reason/humanity” by shifting attention toward animal sentience and moral capacity.

15. A systematic reconstruction: de Waal’s philosophy of mind

Here is the reconstructed system in compressed philosophical form.

First principle: anti-dualistic continuity. There is no scientifically defensible wall separating ensouled human mind from mindless animal mechanism. Evolution applies to cognition, emotion, social intelligence, and morality, not merely to anatomy. Human beings are not less remarkable because other animals have minds; other animals are more remarkable because humans are animals.

Second principle: inferential realism about other minds. Minds are not directly observed, whether human or animal. They are inferred from behavior, expression, physiology, neural homology, evolutionary relationship, and social context. Since we infer human feelings from outward signs, we may infer animal feelings when the evidence is comparably strong.

Third principle: disciplined anthropomorphism. Anthropomorphism is scientifically useful when controlled by natural history, species-typical behavior, phylogenetic proximity, and ecological context. Anthropodenial is as dangerous as projection because it blinds researchers to real continuities.

Fourth principle: embodied affective mind. Emotion is not an irrational residue beneath cognition. It is a bodily, motivational, and organizing dimension of cognition. Fear, attachment, distress, consolation, self-control, and empathy are not “mere behavior”; they are windows into how organisms experience and navigate their worlds.

Fifth principle: ecological pluralism of intelligence. Intelligence is not a single vertical measure defined by human tasks. Each species has cognitive capacities adapted to its own world. The right method is to test animals according to their perceptual, social, and ecological realities.

Sixth principle: graded consciousness. Consciousness is not an all-or-nothing possession. Animals may have different forms and levels of awareness: affective, bodily, social, temporal, and self-related. Planning, episodic memory, self-recognition, and emotional regulation are evidential markers of such awareness.

Seventh principle: relational social mind. Mind is formed and expressed in social life. Coalition, hierarchy, reconciliation, grooming, parenting, consolation, and alliance are not external to cognition; they are major arenas of cognition.

Eighth principle: proto-moral naturalism. Human morality grows out of older capacities: empathy, reciprocity, fairness sensitivity, conflict repair, attachment, and social regulation. Animals need not possess explicit moral doctrines in order to possess proto-moral capacities.

Ninth principle: sentimentalist moral psychology. Moral motivation begins in concern, empathy, aversion, attachment, and social emotion before it becomes explicit principle. Reason matters, but mostly as an elaborator, critic, generalizer, and justifier of older motivational systems.

Tenth principle: religion as cultural elaboration, not moral origin. Religion did not create morality but often organizes, codifies, enforces, narrates, and expands moral tendencies. Theological ideas about souls have also shaped scientific attitudes toward animal minds, sometimes obstructing recognition of animal subjectivity.

16. What this means for “soul”

A de Waalian philosophy cannot affirm “soul” as an exclusive human possession if “soul” means the only source of feeling, agency, sociality, and moral capacity. His work dissolves that exclusivity. But it also need not reduce all talk of soul to nonsense. It pressures theology to clarify what “soul” means.

If soul means inner life, then many animals have something soul-like: affective subjectivity, attachment, suffering, joy, fear, agency, and social awareness. If soul means principle of life, then the older Aristotelian and some Christian hylomorphic traditions have resources for animal souls, although they may still distinguish rational human souls. If soul means moral and spiritual personhood in a full human sense, de Waal would likely say that animals have precursors and partial analogues, but not human symbolic theology, explicit law, or universal moral systems. His science most decisively rejects the idea that animals are soulless in the sense of being inwardly vacant.

Thus, the best theological appropriation of de Waal is not “humans are nothing special.” It is “human specialness cannot be built on animal emptiness.” A theology compatible with de Waal would treat animals as fellow creatures with forms of subjectivity, sociality, and vulnerability. It would also be suspicious of doctrines that justify domination by denying animal mind.

17. Critical tensions and limits

The first limit is epistemic. De Waal admits that feelings cannot be directly measured. He argues by inference, analogy, and parsimony. That is legitimate, but it leaves room for uncertainty about the exact phenomenal character of animal experience. We may have strong reason to believe a chimpanzee feels distress or comfort; we still do not know what chimpanzee distress or comfort is like from the inside.

The second limit is conceptual. Terms such as empathy, fairness, reconciliation, and morality can be stretched too far. The capuchin fairness work is strongest when described as inequity aversion or a precursor to fairness, not as a full human theory of justice. De Waal is usually careful about this, but some popular reception can blur the distinction.

The third limit concerns normativity. Philosophers responding to de Waal have pressed the difference between having pro-social motivations and being bound by moral obligations. A chimpanzee may console, share, punish, or reconcile, but does it understand a norm as a norm? De Waal’s best answer is developmental and genealogical: explicit normativity grows out of proto-normative social expectations, but is not identical with them.

The fourth limit is phylogenetic. De Waal’s confidence is highest with mammals, especially primates and other socially complex mammals. He is open to feeling and cognition in more distant animals, but his own rule implies that the evidential path becomes harder as bodies, expressions, sensory worlds, and evolutionary histories diverge. He explicitly warns, for example, that fish may lack facial expressions without that proving they lack feelings.

The fifth limit is theological. De Waal’s work strongly undermines animal-machine theology and morality-from-religion arguments. It does not settle the existence of God, the metaphysics of the soul, or the full truth-value of any religious tradition. He himself treats the existence of God as unanswerable or outside his main scientific interest, while insisting that science should not use God as an explanatory variable for natural phenomena.

18. Final synthesis

Frans de Waal’s implicit philosophy of mind is a comparative, affective, relational naturalism. It begins from the Darwinian premise that human beings are animals and follows that premise into places where Western thought often hesitated: emotion, morality, consciousness, politics, grief, empathy, and religion. Against behaviorism, he argues that inner life is scientifically discussable when inferred carefully. Against Cartesianism, he rejects the ensouled-human versus animal-machine divide. Against crude anthropomorphism, he insists on species-specific evidence. Against anthropodenial, he insists that refusing similarity can be as unscientific as projecting it.

His view of consciousness is graded rather than binary. His view of mind is embodied rather than disembodied, affective rather than purely rational, social rather than solitary, and ecological rather than human-centered. His view of morality is bottom-up: morality is not a divine or rational veneer placed on savage nature, but an elaboration of older mammalian and primate capacities for empathy, reciprocity, fairness, consolation, and social regulation. His view of religion is similarly genealogical: religion did not invent morality, but it has organized and symbolized moral life, while theological ideas about souls have deeply influenced how humans interpret animal minds.

The most concise statement of the reconstructed system is this: de Waal replaces the exclusive human soul with a continuum of embodied subjectivities. Humans remain distinctive, but not because they alone have mind, feeling, agency, consciousness, or moral roots. They are distinctive because they transform shared animal capacities through language, symbolic culture, institutions, explicit norms, science, and religion. The importance of his work is that it forces philosophy and theology to stop grounding human dignity in the denial of animal inwardness.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
